text:
IMPARTIAL NEWS + INTELLIGENT DEBATE

Account

From a chaotic Olympic Village to the saunas of Roland Garros, athletes are being put last at this Games

Between them, theParis 2024organising committee and nature continue to blight these games. To the rain that scuppered theopening ceremonyand ruined thewater qualityin the Seine, to thecardboard beds, sub-standard food, insufficient toilet facilities and absence of air-conditioning in the Olympic Village that have upset so many athletes, we can add unseasonal heat that turned Roland Garros into a sauna.

After taking the first set against Taylor Fritz of the United States, British No 1 Jack Draper faded badly. Though he was first on court, the midday start afforded little protection in conditions that would later see the mandatory heat rule triggered. When the mercury hits 30.1C, a 10-minute break between the second and third sets is enforced.

Draper, who has not played in comparable heat for at least four months, admitted that he must improve physically to contest matches in this heat. Nevertheless he had a point about the difficulty of rehydrating satisfactorily.

“I mean, we were drinking hot water out there,” he said. “It was hot out there. We were both trying our best to put up some good tennis. Part of it is physicality, but I said to the referee that its pretty poor the players have to drink hot water in those conditions.

“We did have ice towels and stuff but it’s important we have good fridges to keep our water cool. It’s so tough to recover when you are just not replenishing properly. If felt like there was no escape from it [the heat] even at the change of ends.”

Unable to access cold water from fridges the players were forced to use the change of ends to fill up their own bottles at a replenishing station, which ate into the two-minute recovery time between ends.

“I have not played in much hotter conditions than that. Your body really starts to fight against you.

“We just haven’t had a lot of exposure to heat by being in the UK for a while, and being in Europe over the grass [season], we just haven’t experienced this level of heat and humidity. It was tough today and he did a better job of dealing with it than I did.

“You can refill, but at the change of ends I ended up not sitting down because I was refilling the drinks. I want to sit down. At all other tournaments you can get the water from the fridge. At theUS Openthere is a fridge with ice, a cooler.

“I don’t know if it’s normal, but that court [Suzanne Lenglen] does not have any shelter. So even when the umbrella is up the legs are in the sun… I just thought it was pretty poor to put the players through that on court.

“We should not have to fill our water up at the change of ends. We need to sit down. The people in the crowd were struggling just watching and sitting down, let alone playing. And then you have to stand to fill up your water, I just don’t think it’s good enough.”

World No 2Coco Gauff, who lost in the third round to Donna Vekic, complained on TikTok about 10 athletes sharing just two bathrooms. The other nine moved out. Australia’s 400m swimming gold medallist Ariarne Titmus argued that conditions in the village were not conducive to high performance. “Living in the village makes it hard to perform,” she said.

The Australian team had to ship their own produce, including three tonnes of tuna and 2,400 meat pies, to compensate for a food offering in the village that was originally 60 percent vegan. They also brought their own air conditioning units. The rooms in the village have an eco-based water-cooling system but there is no thermostatic temperature control.

Six South Korean swimmers moved into a hotel closer to the Olympic venue to avoid long bus journeys in vehicles without air conditioning. The organisers subsequently address the issue with more additional buses but the sense remains that the Games has placed eco concerns above the performance needs of the athletes.

Impartial news + intelligent debate

title: Jack Draper being given 'hot water' in a heatwave sums up chaos of Paris 2024
author: Kevin Garside
description: From a chaotic Olympic Village to the saunas of Roland Garros, athletes are being put last at this Games
